title: Ruy Lopez: Berlin Defense
summary: Master the solid Berlin Defense, where Black simplifies into an endgame with active piece play.
description: The Berlin Defense is one of the most solid replies to the Ruy Lopez. After the moves 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5 Nf6, Black transposes into the Berlin. The position simplifies quickly, and both sides fight for endgame advantages. This tutorial covers the main line and typical endgame patterns.

variations.endgameLine: 5.Re1 Nd6 6.Bxc6 dxc6 - The typical Berlin endgame structure
variations.modernLine: 5.d4 - The Open Berlin with more complications

objectives: Understand the Berlin structure: why Black gives up the bishop pair for simplification
Learn the typical endgame positions and how to play them
Master the pawn structure: c6, d5, and the isolated e5 pawn
Recognize when White has an advantage and how to convert it
plans: White's plan: Press in the endgame with the extra pawn and better structure
Black's plan: Activate the pieces and create counterplay against White's king
The move ...d5 is Black's main break to free their game
White should avoid exchanging too many pieces - keep some complexity
commonMistakes: Do not think the Berlin is boring - there are many subtle endgame tricks
Avoid taking too many pawns - keep pieces on for winning chances
Do not underestimate Black's piece activity - they play well in the endgame
White should avoid the trap of playing for a win too early
completionSummary: The Berlin Defense is a solid choice. You've learned the main ideas and can navigate the endgame.

steps.title: Claim central space
steps.instruction: Play e4 to control the center.
steps.coaching: The Berlin Defense is one of the oldest defenses to the Ruy Lopez.
steps.hint: Push the king pawn two squares.

steps.title: Black responds symmetrically
steps.coaching: The open game begins. The Berlin is coming.

steps.title: The Ruy Lopez
steps.instruction: Move the bishop to b5, pinning the knight.
steps.coaching: This is the Ruy Lopez, controlling e5 and preparing development.

steps.title: The Berlin move
steps.instruction: The knight develops to f6, attacking e4.
steps.coaching: This is the Berlin Defense! Black attacks e4 immediately.

steps.title: White castles
steps.instruction: Castle kingside, getting the king to safety.
steps.coaching: White castles, preparing to meet the exchange sacrifice.

steps.title: The Berlin Exchange
steps.instruction: Black captures on e4, giving up the bishop pair.
steps.coaching: This is the key position of the Berlin: Black accepts an exchange.

steps.title: Attack the knight
steps.instruction: Move the rook to e1, attacking the knight.
steps.coaching: White attacks! The knight on e4 is now under pressure.

steps.title: Black retreats the knight
steps.coaching: Black retreats, simplifying into the typical Berlin endgame.

steps.title: White simplifies
steps.instruction: Capture on c6 with the bishop.
steps.coaching: White exchanges bishops, entering the Berlin endgame.
steps.hint: Capture on c6 with the bishop.

steps.title: Black recaptures
steps.instruction: Black captures on c6 with the d-pawn.
steps.coaching: This creates the typical Berlin pawn structure: c6, e5.

steps.title: Win a pawn
steps.instruction: Capture on e5 with the knight.
steps.coaching: White wins a pawn. The endgame begins.
steps.hint: Capture on e5 with the knight.

steps.title: Black develops the queen
steps.instruction: The queen moves to e7, attacking the knight.
steps.coaching: Black attacks! The knight on e5 must move.

steps.title: Support the knight
steps.instruction: Play d4 to support the knight on e5.
steps.coaching: White supports the knight and controls central space.

steps.title: Black develops the knight
steps.coaching: Black continues development. The position is now balanced.

steps.title: Support the center
steps.instruction: Play c3 to support the d4 pawn.
steps.coaching: White strengthens the center and prepares development.

steps.title: Black castles
steps.coaching: Both sides are now castled. The endgame phase begins.
